text: The surgeon performs a pediatric laparoscopic appendectomy on a seven-year-old child with acute appendicitis. A small stab wound is made over the inferior crease of the umbilicus, and the abdominal wall is lifted to insert the Veress needle. The insufflator is set to pediatric mode, gradually inflating the abdomen with gas. The preset and actual pressures on the insufflator are monitored until they align, indicating complete insufflation. The Veress needle is removed, and the trocar is carefully introduced into the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon carefully removes the Veress needle after insufflation is complete, then introduces the trocar through the umbilical port. The laparoscopic view reveals a lump formation in the right iliac fossa with phlegmon and omentum adhered over it. Gentle blunt dissection is performed to separate the appendix from the abdominal wall, followed by the use of a harmonic scalpel to separate the appendix and omentum adhesions.

The surgeon introduces the first umbilical port and inserts the telescope, revealing a lump formation in the right iliac fossa with phlegmon and adhered omentum. Gentle blunt dissection is performed to separate the appendix from the abdominal wall, followed by the use of a harmonic scalpel to dissect the major appendix and free the omentum. The procedure utilizes a 5mm port for the harmonic scalpel and a 3mm contralateral port for pediatric instruments.
The surgeon uses a 5mm port for the harmonic scalpel and a 3mm contralateral port, both pediatric instruments, to slowly dissect the major appendix and free the omentum.

The surgeon separates omental adhesions, lifting them to avoid contact with surrounding bowel loops. The base of the appendix is skeletonized, revealing the cecum near the teniae coli and the ileocecal junction. The harmonic scalpel is used for dissection, with the Teflon jaw positioned towards the appendix to prevent perforation.

The surgeon separates omental adhesions, lifting them to avoid contact with surrounding bowel loops. The base of the pediatric appendix is skeletonized, revealing the cecum near the teniae coli and the ileocecal junction. The harmonic scalpel is used carefully, with the Teflon jaw positioned towards the appendix to prevent perforation, as the base of the appendix is reached.

The surgeon addresses minor oozing around the surgical site with suction and irrigation. Mishra's knot, an extracorporeal slip knot, is used to ligate the appendix. The knot is configured with multiple hitches, winds, and locks, applied using a Vandarkar reusable knot pusher with a silicon tip to prevent tissue crushing and suture breakage. The knot pusher is positioned at the base of the appendix, and the appendix is successfully ligated before the suture is cut.

The surgeon addresses minor oozing around the surgical site by suctioning and irrigating the area. Mishra's knot, an extracorporeal slip knot, is used to tie the appendix. The knot configuration includes multiple hitches, winds, and locks. A Vandarkar reusable knot pusher with a silicon tip is utilized to prevent tissue crushing and suture breakage. The knot pusher is positioned at the base of the appendix, and the appendix is successfully ligated. The knot pusher is then removed, and the suture is cut.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the appendix approximately five millimeters from the knot, sealing the distal end to prevent contamination. A 5mm telescope is positioned in the left iliac fossa, and a 10mm umbilical port is used to introduce an endo bag into the abdomen. The endo bag is opened, and the appendix is placed inside before being extracted through the umbilical port. The umbilical port is then closed using a cobbler's needle, ensuring all layers except the skin are sutured.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to divide the appendix approximately five millimeters away from the knot, effectively burning the mucosa and sealing the distal end to prevent contamination. A 5mm telescope is positioned in the left iliac fossa, and a 10mm umbilical port is used to introduce an endo bag into the abdomen. The endo bag is opened, and the appendix is placed inside before being extracted through the umbilical port. The umbilical port is then closed using a cobbler's needle, ensuring all layers except the skin are sutured.
